metadata.title: Beyond Moral Development: Re-Theorizing Ethical Practices in Service Learning.
metadata.description: The author calls for a re-conceptualization of the philosophical framework behind the pedagogical practice of service learning based on the theories of Paul Ricoeur, who emphasizes the role of narrative in the development of the ethical self. He argues that the preoccupation of most of the service learning literature with assessing the

metadata.title: Psalm Recitation and Post-Secular Time: Augustine, the Ipod, and Psalm 90.
metadata.description: The author encourages Biblical scholars to take into closer consideration the traditions of recitation and reception of the Book of Psalms, especially Psalm 90, in order for them to gain a more nuanced understanding of how these psalms imagine the nature of time itself. He observes how the Psalms are an anthology arranged according to different traditions of recitation and practice, and adds that St. Augustine used psalm recitation as an analogy to describe the workings of time and memory.

metadata.title: Education as Event: a Conversation with John D. Caputo.

metadata.title: Silence, Prayer, and Desire in Pedagogical Practices: a Conversation with Sarah Coakley.
metadata.description: An interview with American theologian Sarah Coakley on how spiritual practices like silent prayer inform her pedagogical practice is presented. Coakley shares that while she tries to make time for silent prayer amidst her numerous teaching commitments, such practices must not be included as a requirement in theological courses. Citing George Steiner's comments on the eroticism inherent in the teacher-student relationship, she warns theology teachers to be on guard against self-aggrandizement.

metadata.title: Three Classic Readings of ‘Ethnographic Practice’ in Religious Studies‘
Three Classroom Readings of ‘Ethnographic Practice’ in Religious Studies.
metadata.description: The author looks at several models of ethnographic practices of fieldwork and writing and their possible contributions to religious studies. She notes that American anthropologist Clifford Geertz favored a cultural-interpretivist approach to ethnography, while French philosopher Michel de Certeau observes that the value of ethnographic writing lies in its re-inscription of alterity. metadata.title: Slow Reading: Learning the Time of the Body
Slow Reading: Learning in the Time of the Body.
metadata.description: The author calls attention to the fracturing of temporality caused by the distractions that go hand-in-hand with 21st century life, which she claims has had a detrimental impact on the ability of students to apply themselves to activities requiring close textual attention. Thus, she urges educators to adapt some of the practices of medieval monastic communities in the modern classroom by modeling to their students the pleasures involved in attending to texts in a focused yet sensual manner.

metadata.title: Jesus Beyond Christ: Erri De Luca and the ‘Self as Stumbling Block’
Teaching for Social Justice: Creating a Context for Education
Teaching for Social Justice: Creating a Context for Transformation.
metadata.description: The author explains how she applies the principles of transformative pedagogy described in the writings of Brazilian educator Paulo Freire in teaching ethics to American college students. She relates that rather than striving to indoctrinate her students with her own values, she employs the spiral of theory and praxis to come up with experiential learning activities that will help them think about how their actions could have a positive or negative impact on the communities in which they live.

metadata.title: Recalling Our Vocation: a Conversation About Teaching with Mark C. Taylor.
metadata.description: An interview with U.S. educator and philosopher of religion Mark C. Taylor on the continuing relevance of pedagogy in the 21st century is presented. Taylor observes that the existing model for university, first conceived by German philosopher Immanuel Kant, is becoming obsolete with the increasing cost of higher education. However, he believes that liberal arts education is more valuable than ever since it allows students to learn more about cultures and traditions different from their own.
